claim: 5% of perinatal mortality is due to low birth weight.

result.claim: 5% of perinatal mortality is due to low birth weight.
result.reasoning: The knowledge base does not contain any direct evidence to support or refute the claim that 5% of perinatal mortality is due to low birth weight. Searches for propositions and chunks related to the claim, including variations in phrasing, did not yield any specific percentages or direct causal links quantifying the proportion of perinatal mortality attributable to low birth weight. While some papers discuss perinatal mortality rates (e.g., Paper ID: 7662395) and the risk of low birth weight (e.g., Paper ID: 2425364), none provide the specific statistical relationship stated in the claim.

evidence.text: Relative to women who weighed 3.000-3.499 kg, the risk was 0.96 in those who weighed < 2.500 kg in birth record data.
evidence.source: Birth Size and Breast Cancer Risk: Re-analysis of Individual Participant Data from 32 Studies

evidence.text: The perinatal mortality rate was 69 per 1000 births.
evidence.source: Perinatal mortality in rural China: retrospective cohort study.

evidence.text: Birth weight was positively associated with breast cancer risk in studies based on birth records.
evidence.source: Birth Size and Breast Cancer Risk: Re-analysis of Individual Participant Data from 32 Studies

evidence.text: Pregnant women with low serum 25-OHD levels had an increased risk of low birthweight infants.
evidence.source: Association between maternal serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D level and pregnancy and neonatal outcomes: systematic review and meta-analysis of observational studies.

evidence.text: Studies involving five categories of birth weight identified odds ratios (ORs) of 1.24 (95% confidence interval [CI] 1.04 to 1.48) for 4,000 g or more.
evidence.source: Intrauterine environments and breast cancer risk: meta-analysis and systematic review

evidence.text: The confidence interval for risk was 0.80-1.16 for women weighing less than 2.500 kg in birth record data.
evidence.source: Birth Size and Breast Cancer Risk: Re-analysis of Individual Participant Data from 32 Studies

evidence.text: Increased birthweight was associated with a relative risk of 1.15 (95% CI 1.09-1.21) for breast cancer.
evidence.source: Intrauterine factors and risk of breast cancer: a systematic review and meta-analysis of current evidence.

evidence.text: Pooled relative risk per one standard deviation increment in birth weight was 1.06 in studies based on birth records.
evidence.source: Birth Size and Breast Cancer Risk: Re-analysis of Individual Participant Data from 32 Studies

evidence.text: The early neonatal mortality was 29 in boys per 1000 live births.
evidence.source: Perinatal mortality in rural China: retrospective cohort study.

evidence.text: The 95% confidence interval for those who weighed > or = 4.000 kg was 1.00-1.25 in birth record data.
evidence.source: Birth Size and Breast Cancer Risk: Re-analysis of Individual Participant Data from 32 Studies

evidence.text: Studies based on three birth weight categories showed an odds ratio (OR) of 1.15 (95% CI 1.01 to 1.31) for > or =4,000 g relative to <3,000 g.
evidence.source: Intrauterine environments and breast cancer risk: meta-analysis and systematic review

evidence.text: Studies involving five categories of birth weight identified odds ratios (ORs) of 1.15 (95% CI 1.04 to 1.26) for 3,500 g to 3,999 g.
evidence.source: Intrauterine environments and breast cancer risk: meta-analysis and systematic review

evidence.text: In both sexes, mortality was lowest at about 22.5-25 kg/m(2).
evidence.source: Body-mass index and cause-specific mortality in 900 000 adults: collaborative analyses of 57 prospective studies.

evidence.text: The studies used a birth weight of 2,500 to 2,599 g as a reference.
evidence.source: Intrauterine environments and breast cancer risk: meta-analysis and systematic review

evidence.text: The rate of stillbirth was 24 per 1000 births.
evidence.source: Perinatal mortality in rural China: retrospective cohort study.

evidence.text: The early neonatal mortality was 69 in girls per 1000 live births.
evidence.source: Perinatal mortality in rural China: retrospective cohort study.

evidence.text: In birth record data, p for linear trend = 0.001 in those who weighed >= 4.000 kg.
evidence.source: Birth Size and Breast Cancer Risk: Re-analysis of Individual Participant Data from 32 Studies

evidence.text: The relative risk estimate for breast cancer comparing women with high birthweight to women with low birthweight combining all studies including both pre- and postmenopausal breast cancer was 1.23 with a 95% confidence interval of 1.13 to 1.34.
evidence.source: Role of birthweight in the etiology of breast cancer.

evidence.text: Relative to women who weighed 3.000-3.499 kg, the risk was 1.12 in those who weighed > or = 4.000 kg in birth record data.
evidence.source: Birth Size and Breast Cancer Risk: Re-analysis of Individual Participant Data from 32 Studies

evidence.text: Studies based on two birth weight categories showed an odds ratio (OR) of 1.09 (95% CI 1.02 to 1.18) for > or =3,000 g relative to <3,000 g.
evidence.source: Intrauterine environments and breast cancer risk: meta-analysis and systematic review

evidence.text: The early neonatal mortality was 46 per 1000 live births.
evidence.source: Perinatal mortality in rural China: retrospective cohort study.

evidence.text: A 5 kg/m(2) higher BMI was associated with 20% higher mortality from all other causes (HR 1.20 [1.16-1.25]).
evidence.source: Body-mass index and cause-specific mortality in 900 000 adults: collaborative analyses of 57 prospective studies.
